trajectory.description: Observation: 100 epochs of DKT reached val_auc=0.7611 (best), but plateaued around epoch 40-50 at ~0.758. The model is clearly stuck in a local optimum. Analysis: The basic DKT with LSTM is limited. I should try a transformer-based approach (SAKT - Self-Attentive Knowledge Tracing) which typically outperforms LSTM-based DKT. Also, I should try: 1) larger model, 2) different input representation. The key issue is the model plateaued early. Let me implement SAKT with attention mechanism which should give better performance. Also, I notice the best checkpoint was at epoch ~40 with 0.7611. Decision: Implement a transformer-based Knowledge Tracing model (similar to SAKT/AKT) with multi-head attention. This should significantly improve over LSTM-based DKT.

trajectory.description: Observation: SAKT only got 0.7164 vs DKT's 0.7611. The transformer is overfitting. Analysis: The DKT LSTM model is better for this dataset. Let me improve DKT with: 1) larger model (hidden_dim=256, embed_dim=128), 2) better regularization, 3) proper learning rate schedule with warmup, 4) longer training. Also, I should fix the SAKT - the issue is the exercise embedding query should be skill_ids[:, 1:] (the next skill to predict), not skill_ids[:, t]. Let me implement a proper SAKT and also improve DKT. Actually, let me focus on what works: improve DKT significantly.

trajectory.description: Observation: DKT plateaus at ~0.761 val_auc. SAKT underperformed. The best checkpoint is from iter 3 with 0.7611. Analysis: Need a fundamentally better approach. The one-hot input representation is limiting. Let me implement a proper transformer-based KT model with learned embeddings. The key insight is: use separate skill embeddings + response embeddings, combine them, then use a causal transformer. This is similar to AKT but simpler. Also, I should try training DKT with much lower learning rate and more epochs since it was still improving slowly. Decision: Implement a clean transformer KT model with learned embeddings and causal attention. Also fix the logging issue (iter_5 shows empty curves - need to fix metrics logging).

trajectory.description: Observation: DKT peaks at 0.7606 val_auc consistently. The cosine LR schedule causes overfitting after epoch 13. Analysis: The model needs better regularization and LR scheduling. The key issue is the LR schedule is too aggressive - it warms up then decays, causing the model to overfit. Let me try: 1) ReduceLROnPlateau scheduler instead of cosine, 2) stronger dropout=0.5, 3) lower initial LR=5e-4, 4) fix metrics logging. Also, I should try a different approach - maybe the DKT with 2 layers and hidden_dim=256 is already optimal, and I need to focus on better regularization. Let me also try label smoothing and gradient clipping.

Key strategies: 1) Much stronger regularization (dropout=0.5, weight_decay=1e-4), 2) Smaller model to reduce capacity, 3) Very low LR. Also, I should try a completely different approach - maybe use a simpler model that doesn't overfit. Let me also try training with a very small LR (1e-4) and no LR decay to see if the model can converge to a better optimum without overfitting. The best result so far is 0.7611 from iter 3. I need to beat this. Let me try: hidden_dim=256, embed_dim=128, dropout=0.5, lr=1e-4, weight_decay=1e-4, no LR decay (just constant LR), 200 epochs with patience=50.

trajectory.description: Observation: DKT consistently peaks at ~0.760-0.761 val_auc. The model overfits after ~27 epochs regardless of regularization. Analysis: I need a fundamentally different approach. The original DKT paper used one-hot input vectors of size 2*num_skills. Let me try: 1) GRU with one-hot input (original DKT), 2) Try a different architecture. Also, I should try an ensemble of multiple models. Let me implement the original DKT with one-hot input and also try a model with more capacity but stronger regularization. The key insight is that learned embeddings might be overfitting - one-hot input forces the model to learn from scratch without embedding overfitting. Also, let me try using variational dropout (same dropout mask across time steps) which is known to help LSTMs generalize better.
